skos:Concept.skos:scopeNote: A purple or violet variety of quartz often used in jewelry. Amethyst is a semi-precious stone which owes its violet colour to irridation, iron impurities, and the presence of trace elements, which result in complex crystal lattice substitutions.
Hard, transparent, often coloured mineral. Can be found on its own as crystals and veins but also as a major constituent of rocks such as granite. Often used decoratively.
A material made from extremely fine fibres of glass and is used as a reinforcing agent for many polymer products.
Materials used in the manufacture of objects.
A soft, lustrous low-melting crystalline metallic element that is malleable and ductile at ordinary temperatures and that is used as a protective coating, in tinfoil, and in soft solders and alloys.
A precious metal of lustrous white colour with great malleability and ductility.
Blue-white metal, obtained from ore, and used commercially for roofing, galvanizing iron, or in alloys. Also used in the 19th century as sheathing for ship's hulls.
A precious metal characterized by its yellow colour and resistance to tarnishing.
A white ductile metal used, for example, in alloys, for instruments, and in aircraft construction.
Metal used primarily for roof coverings and window fittings. It is easy to work, can be worked cold and does not rust which makes it suitable for holding or repelling water.
A metallic substance made by combining two or more elements at least one of which is a metal. Use more specific terms where known.
Metal used primarily for roofing and ornamental purposes, being lighter and stronger than lead.
A metallic element often used in alloys and historically used in pottery and glass-making, paints, enamelling, and in cosmetics and medicine. Often derived from its sulphite ore, stibnite.

Class of elements and alloys that are characteristically lustrous,ductile, fusible and malleable. These are extracted from ore minerals originally existing in nature and processed before becoming a recognisable metal. See METAL Class List for narrow terms
A malleable, ductile ferromagnetic metal widely used for structural and decorative purposes.
Natural inorganic substance possessing a definite chemical composition in a crystalline form. See MINERAL Class List for narrow terms.

Alabaster is a name applied to varieties of two distinct minerals: gypsum and calcite. The finer kinds of alabaster are employed as an ornamental stone, especially for internal decoration as it's softness allows it to be carved into elaborate forms.
A coloured, glass-like substance (opaque or partially opaque) that is fused to the surface of metal or glass or pottery for decorative or protective purposes.
North American wood, the name being applied either to the American Larch; or to the Black or Ridge-pole Pine of inland areas; and the Scrub Pine of maritime regions.
A hard, compact, unprocessed, fibrous cellulose substance. The roots, trunks and branches of trees and shrubs consist of this tissue.

Dark and dense hardwood timber which may be sourced from various species of true ebony from southern Asia and Africa, but the name may also be given to dark hardwoods from elsewhere, e.g. the Americas.
The wood of the evergreen coniferous tree characterized by single needle-like leaves and errect cones.
A strong durable wood originating in India and south-east Asia.
A hard, durable wood obtained from trees of the genus Ulmus.
A light, soft, moderately-strong wood obtained from trees of the genus Picea.
A straight-grained, durable and often resinous timber obtained from trees of the genus Pinus.
The timber from genus Betula, found in Europe and North America, particularly in northern regions.
A deciduous conifer native to Britain and Europe supplying durable and waterproof timber suitable for shipbuilding features such as masts.
A native hardwood of the Beech family with hard and durable characteristics which traditionally made it appropriate for structural work.
A commonly coppiced wood from the genus Corylus.
Rock of definite form and size, usually artificially shaped.
A coarse grained metamorphic rock that is composed maily of green, brown or black amphibole minerals and plagioclase feldspar. Amphiboles are usually members of the hornblende group.
Pertaining to or deriving from a living organism. See ANIMAL Class list for narrow terms.
Hard, protective covering made largely of calcium salts secreted by soft bodied invertebrate animals.
Metal alloy with lead as the principal component.
Soft, very fine grained, dark coloured, sedimentary rock which splits easily.
Medium grained, hard, white/grey coloured stone consisting of quartz and often silica, produced by the metamorphosis of sandstone.
Hard, durable, dark grey stone, the purest native form of silica, which can either be used decoratively or as a main material. Predominantly found in East Anglia and the South East.

A clay-based ceramic fired at low kiln temperatures (c.800-1200 degrees). Essentially, earthenwares are porous and require glazing for most applications. This term has been used interchangeably with TERRACOTTA and with POTTERY
A ceramic made from clay and fired in a kiln or oven. This term has been used interchangeably with EARTHENWARE and with TERRACOTTA
A sedimentary rock primarily composed of silt sized particles.
Elastic polymeric substance made from the latex of a tropical plant or synthetically
A hard, coarse grained, durable igneous rock. Can be used decoratively or as a main material. Found throughout the country.
Coarse basaltic rock.
Easily formed metal alloy composed of copper and zinc.
A hard, translucent variety of jade which is rarer than the other varieties of nephrite as a result considered to be the best and very desirable.
Sedimentary rock consisting mainly of calcium carbonate. Varying in grain size, colour and hardness, quarried and used throughout the country.
Geologist's term for any dark coloured, slightly metamorphosed igneous rock. Index with specific stone type where given.
A thin, flexible material made from the pulp of wood or other fibrous matter which is then pressed and dried to form sheets.

A hydrated aluminium phosphate mineral sometimes used as a semi-precious stone, and for carvings and ornamental use. Named after Variscia, the historical name of the Vogtland in Germany. Mainly green in colour.
Transparent, hard substance made by fusing soda-silica which solidifies from a molten state.
Any of the pieces of hard tissue consisting largely of calcium phosphate that make up the skeleton of a vertebrate animal.
A form of bone, which grows on the heads of members of the deer family and is shed annually. Antler can be used by man for a variety of purposes
Horn is a hollow, bone-like projection consisting mainly of keratin, found on certain animals (sheep, cows, goats etc.)

A conglomerate rock comprising well rounded flint pebbles in a very hard matrix of fine pale coloured sand, bound together by hard natural silica cement. The pebbles are usually coloured ochre or red and have black rims.
Conglomerate consisting of rounded flint pebbles held together in a natural silica and sand cement.
An alloy which is composed predominantly of tin (c.85-99 percent).
A green coloured gemstone. A variety of the mineral beryl coloured green by trace amounts of chromium and sometimes vanadium.
A silicate mineral used since the Bronze Age as gemstones and abrasives. Found in many colours including red, orange, yellow, green, purple, brown, blue, black, pink and colourless.
An early plastic formed from a condensation reaction of phenol with formaldehyde which was developed by Leo Baekeland in 1907.
Soft, white, porous sedimentary rock, a form of limestone composed of the mineral calcite.
A hard, black form of lignite that takes a brilliant polish. It is used in jewellery and for ornamentation.
A hard, white, fine-grained substance forming the main part of the tusks of elephants, walruses, hippos and narwhals.
A hard, bone-like material obtained from animal teeth.
A brownish-red mineral commonly used as a semi-precious gemstone. A variety of the silica mineral chalcedony coloured by impurities of iron oxide.

Collective name for, in a true geological sense, a metamorphosed limestone, eg. Carrara marble, and, in the British sense any decorative stone that will take a polish, eg. Purbeck marble. In the latter's case index with specific stone type where given
A strong, durable alloy of iron and carbon.
Onyx is a cryptocrystalline form of quartz. It is a semi-precious stone that is usually cut as a cabochon, or into beads, and is also used for intaglios and cameos.
Hard, corrosive alloy of copper and tin.
An ornamental green stone of the mineral nephrite.
A fine grained earth which becomes more plastic when water is added and can be moulded and dried to make bricks, tile, pottery, etc.
A building material used for coating walls and ceilings. Plaster can refer to gypsum plaster (also known as plaster of Paris), lime plaster, or cement plaster.
A form of volcanic glass with similar properties to FLINT and often used in the manufacture of sharp-edged tools.
A hard, igneous rock formed from the cooling of molten volcanic magma.
The black, lightweight and porous residue of partly burnt wood.
An alloy where the predominant component metal is copper.
A semi-precious, fossilised resin usually used for ornamentation and jewellery.
Animal skin that has been hardened or treated by a tanning process.
A hard silica rich rock, black or brown coloured, commonly found in nodular form.
Medium to coarse grained metamorphic rock, colour being determined by its dominant mineral. Often used for floor and wall cladding.
Crystalline allotropic form of carbon used in pencils, as a lubricant etc.
A sedimantary rock composed of fine grained clay particles. Flaky mudstone is called shale.
Hard, unglazed, brown, versatile earthenware which can be used as a tile or moulded into statuary.
A hard material formed by firing clay. For pottery, use POTTERY.
Use for objects made from coal or for stray finds of coal where the association with a wreck site is not proven and thus cannot be stated as either cargo or bunkers.

Composed of sand, stones or other aggregate and cement mixed with water which sets in a hard mass. The material is usually associated with modern building but was developed by Romans where cement was obtained from quicklime.

Collective term for a hard, fine grained, dark coloured metamorphic rock, which splits easily; hence its common use as a roofing and flooring material, and a functional description for any rectangular, sheet, roofing material.
A glass-like compound first made in ancient Egypt. It involves coating a core material of powdered quartz with a vitreous alkaline glaze. Also the name given to a tin-glazed earthenware.

Originally obtained by burning limestone to produce quicklime. Now a fine grey powder made from a mixture of limestone and clay used with water and sand to make mortar or with water, sand and aggregate to make concrete.

Geologically sandstone consists of particles of quartz naturally cemented together by either silica, calcium or iron. It is this geological composition that accounts for the varying colours and grain sizes found in the many building sandstones.
